title: Explore Simpler Eigenmarking: Quantum Entailment Model Checking
abstract: Targeting entailment model checking, a recent study has pioneered an idea of Eigenmarking search, an improvement over Grover search using extra qubits. The extra qubits condition the quantum state evolution such that the answer states (if exist) are always in the minority. The minority criteria is essential to Grover probability-amplitude amplification and consequently the effectiveness of Grover search. In addition to enforce the minority criteria, Eigenmarking also employs complementary states (through well-orchestrated phase rotation) for easy identification of a no-answer case (related to a no-violation case in the context of model checking). Eigenmarking search has been shown effective in two-qubit simulations.   The three Eigenmarking schemes have been previously proposed. Two schemes require two extra qubits. One scheme (called ``subtle marking'') requires one extra qubit with a multiple-qubit-controlled phase rotation. Our study refines the mechanism using only one extra qubit with only two-qubit-controlled phase rotation, commonly known as \texttt{ccz}, regardless of how many qubits the input has. Using a multiple-qubit-controlled phase rotation (as in subtle marking) associates with highly entangled states. Highly entangled states in a real quantum hardware are difficult (or in some cases may even be unachievable) particularly in a scaled up scenario involving many qubits. Our proposed new Eigenmarking scheme has lightened the burden for the hardware requirement. The new Eigenmarking search has been experimented in two-qubit-system simulations and shown viable, achieving the minimal relative local winning margin of W=3.17 and the worst-case distinguishability of D=0.769 (cf. W=0.67; D=0.19 from conventional marking and W=0.28; D=0.55 from subtle marking).
summary: This paper proposes a more efficient version of the Eigenmarking quantum search algorithm designed for checking logical entailment. By replacing complex multi-qubit controlled rotations with simpler two-qubit CCZ gates, the author reduces the hardware burden for quantum computers. Simulations show that this simpler scheme provides much better distinguishability and a higher winning margin than previous methods. This makes the algorithm more viable for implementation on near-term quantum hardware.
main_problem: The paper aims to optimize the 'Eigenmarking' search algorithm for quantum entailment model checking by reducing the hardware complexity of required quantum gates.
main_result: The proposed 'Simpler Eigenmarking' scheme achieves significantly higher winning margins and better distinguishability using only two-qubit controlled phase rotations (CCZ gates), regardless of input size.
method: The study refines the 'subtle marking' algorithm by replacing multi-qubit-controlled phase rotations with simpler CCZ gates and evaluates the performance using Qiskit-based two-qubit system simulations.
model_or_system: The research utilizes a quantum computing framework based on Grover's algorithm, specifically simulating a three-qubit system consisting of input qubits, a tag qubit, and an ancillary qubit.
important_parameters: The relative local winning margin (W) and the worst-case distinguishability (D).
main_assumptions: The results are based on two-qubit system simulations; scalability to many qubits and performance on real, noisy quantum hardware remain unproven.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows classical logic gate simulation; Figure 2 displays the circuit diagram for the Simpler Eigenmarking system; Figure 3 shows experimental counts for target states; Figure 4 and 5 illustrate the implementation and results of entailment checking.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of entailment model checking, reviews existing Eigenmarking schemes (Conventional and Subtle), proposes a refined CCZ-based scheme, presents simulation results comparing performance metrics, and discusses scalability and hardware implications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIGURE 1. Quantum gate applications: (a) logical AND, (b) its simulated result, (c) logical OR, and (d) its simulated result.
FIGURE 2. Simpler Eigenmarking. Generic diagram (left) and Qiskit graphic draw of a three-qubit system (right): x2 is the most significant qubit (msq).
FIGURE 3. Plots of experimental results on a two-qubit system simulation. A target state has prefix 01.

Figure 4 shows diagrams implemented entailment model checking for an example described in § Background. The simulation results are shown in Figure 5. Note that β3 and β4 introduces more logical symbols, hence more states.
FIGURE 4. Diagrams: knowledge α : {A ⇒B,B ⇒C} (left), sentence β1 : A ⇒C (middle), and entailment checking (right).
FIGURE 5. Entailment checking simulation. The decisive logic is shown as the first digit, e.g., β1 has no violation: all y’s is 1’s.
